person.prominent_name_reason: Charles Olcott (1793-1857) — pioneer Medina Ohio lawyer, abolitionist author, and inventor of the metallic (iron) ship (patented 1835). His book "A Blow at Slavery" was read aloud in Congress. Great-great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: Charles Olcott and Betsey Mix Cowles were Hooker descendants active.

notable.notable_blurb: Abolitionist whose lectures were read in Congress

narrative_blocks.header: His abolitionist lectures were read aloud in Congress
narrative_blocks.body: His 1838 'Two Lectures on Slavery and Abolition' was read aloud in Congress as evidence for the unconstitutional nature of slavery. Edward Hooker called him 'a clear, bold writer, and a great Anti-Slavery man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Patented iron ship 1835; Navy rejected as impractical
narrative_blocks.body: On July 13, 1835 he patented an iron ship — 'a new and useful improvement in ship building and naval architecture, consisting in constructing ships wholly of iron.' The Naval board rejected it as 'entirely impractical,' but the Navy built iron ships shortly after; he never received compensation.

narrative_blocks.header: Mentored Joel Tiffany — refrigerator car inventor
narrative_blocks.body: He mentored Joel Tiffany, a Connecticut-born law student who later invented the refrigerator railroad car and authored abolitionist legal theory. Both Olcott and Tiffany were part of Medina's network of Connecticut-rooted reformers in the 1830s-50s.

education.notes: — Yale 1816. EH incorrectly states 1805 (impossible — born 1793). Pioneer Medina Ohio lawyer, abolitionist, iron ship patent holder 1835. Son of Cynthia Hooker (H00310) and Rev. Allen Olcott (Yale 1768 — also in Hooker line circle). Two Yale graduates in the same Olcott family, father and son.

parents.father_research_notes: Captain Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.
parents.mother_research_notes: Penelope Beckwith Olcott, wife of Capt. Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.

burial.plot_notes: Bolton Center Cemetery, Bolton, Tolland County, Connecticut. Cenotaphic inscription also on Olcott family monument in West Cemetery, Manchester CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Yale-educated 'quaint and erratic' preacher
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia married Rev. Allen Olcott in 1792 — a Yale College 1768 graduate settled at the Farmington Presbyterian Church in 1787. After four years his preaching was deemed 'quaint and erratic' enough that he was dismissed and never settled at any other church.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled with Allen on a South Manchester farm
narrative_blocks.body: After his Farmington dismissal Allen returned to his native East Hartford and then settled with Cynthia on a farm at South Manchester, Connecticut. Family stories suggest his farming methods were as unique as his preaching had been. He died there in April 1811, leaving Cynthia a widow with five children ranging from eight to eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Capt. Samuel Alvord at fifty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Allen's death Cynthia remarried — Capt. Samuel Alvord of East Hartford, on 14 January 1813. The couple lived at East Hartford until her own death in June 1827. Through her first marriage's daughter Cynthia, her line married back into the Talcott-Hooker convergence.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

crossConnections.display_label: Charles Olcott and Betsey Mix Cowles were Hooker descendants active.
